title: The 19th hole: nassau, skins, and friendly money
microFact: A 'nassau' is three bets in one: front nine, back nine, overall. Stakes are small; the ritual is the point.
lesson:
Golf's social games, so you can nod along and join in:

• NASSAU: the classic. Three equal bets — front 9, back 9, 18 — usually played as match play with handicaps. "₹100 nassau?" means ₹300 max exposure.
• PRESS: when losing a nassau segment, you may 'press' — open a new bet on the remaining holes. Doubling-down culture; accept or decline gracefully.
• SKINS: each hole is worth a 'skin'; lowest score takes it, ties carry the value forward until someone wins outright.
• SNAKE: whoever three-putts holds the snake; last holder at 18 pays. Watch putting sharpen instantly.

Etiquette for the newcomer: play if invited and stakes are trivial (they usually are — this is ritual, not gambling); know YOUR handicap so strokes can be set; pay up immediately and cheerfully at the bar; never relitigate a ruling over drinks.

And the 19th hole itself: buy a round when you win, linger even when you lose. The community you're trying to join does its actual bonding here, not on the course.

quiz.question: You're 3 down on the front nine of a nassau and feeling brave. What can you do?
options: Press — open an additional bet on the remaining holes
quiz.explanation: A press starts a fresh bet on the holes left, giving the loser a comeback path. Accepting a press is near-obligatory in the culture.
